status_notes: Initial research from GCP Compute Engine documentation, 2026-04-04. Needs cross-reference pass and human review with live GCP environment.
notes: Virtual machines for GCP. Security includes shielded VMs, confidential VMs, OS Login, metadata protection, sole-tenant nodes.

match_nodes.description: Secure boot + vTPM + integrity monitoring for VM instances

data.description: Shielded VMs provide verifiable integrity of VM instances. Secure boot ensures only authenticated software runs during boot. vTPM enables measured boot and provides a virtual Trusted Platform Module. Integrity monitoring compares boot measurements against a known-good baseline.

options.description: Validates digital signatures of all boot components. Blocks unsigned or tampered boot code.
Virtual TPM 2.0 for measured boot, disk encryption key sealing, and attestation.
Compares boot measurements against baseline. Alerts via Cloud Monitoring on drift.

default.note: Available for most machine types. Enabled by default for new instances using Shielded VM images.

data.vendor_recommendation: Enable Shielded VM for all production instances. Use Shielded VM images from Google or create custom Shielded images.

match_nodes.description: Hardware-based memory encryption using AMD SEV or Intel TDX

data.description: Confidential VMs encrypt data in memory using hardware-based encryption keys managed by the processor. The hypervisor and host OS cannot access the VM's memory contents. Provides cryptographic attestation.

options.name: AMD SEV
options.description: AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization. N2D machine types.

options.name: AMD SEV-SNP
options.description: AMD SEV with Secure Nested Paging. Enhanced isolation.

options.name: Intel TDX
options.description: Intel Trust Domain Extensions. C3 machine types.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use Confidential VMs for workloads processing highly sensitive data. Combine with CMEK for disk encryption and VPC Service Controls for network isolation.

match_nodes.description: IAM-based SSH access management replacing SSH key management

data.description: OS Login uses Google IAM to manage SSH access to instances. Maps Google identities to Linux user accounts. Eliminates the need to manage SSH keys in project or instance metadata. Supports 2FA requirement.

options.name: OS Login
options.description: IAM-based SSH access. Roles: roles/compute.osLogin (standard) or roles/compute.osAdminLogin (sudo).

options.name: OS Login with 2FA
options.description: Requires second factor for SSH sessions. Uses Google 2-Step Verification.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use OS Login instead of managing SSH keys. Enable 2FA for production instances. Disable project-wide SSH keys when using OS Login.

match_nodes.description: Project and instance metadata access controls

data.description: Instance metadata contains sensitive information including service account tokens, SSH keys, and startup scripts. Metadata server (169.254.169.254) is accessible from within the instance. Controls include blocking project-wide SSH keys and restricting metadata access.
data.vendor_recommendation: Block project-wide SSH keys on sensitive instances. Use OS Login instead of metadata-based SSH keys. Restrict metadata access in custom images.

match_nodes.description: IAM identity for VM instances — runtime credential provider

data.description: Service accounts provide Google Cloud IAM identity for VM instances. Applications on the VM use the service account's credentials to authenticate to Google Cloud APIs. The default compute service account has broad Editor permissions and is discouraged for production.

options.name: Default compute service account
options.description: Automatically created. Has Editor role (broad permissions). Discouraged for production.

options.name: Custom service account
options.description: User-created with specific, least-privilege IAM roles.

options.name: No service account
options.description: Instance has no Google Cloud credentials. Cannot call Google APIs.

data.vendor_recommendation: Use custom service accounts with minimal permissions. Never use default compute service account in production. Limit service account scopes.

data.description: VPC firewall rules control incoming and outgoing traffic to VM instances. Rules are stateful and can target instances by network tags, service accounts, or all instances in the network. Evaluated by priority (lower number = higher priority).
data.vendor_recommendation: Use least-privilege firewall rules. Prefer service account-based targeting over network tags. Use hierarchical firewall policies for organization-wide rules.

match_nodes.description: Dedicated physical servers for compliance and licensing requirements

data.description: Sole-tenant nodes are physical servers dedicated to hosting only your VMs. No other customers share the hardware. Used for compliance requirements (data isolation), licensing (bring-your-own-license), and workloads requiring physical isolation.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use sole-tenant nodes when compliance or licensing requires dedicated hardware. Combine with Shielded VMs and Confidential VMs for maximum isolation.

data.description: All data written to persistent disks is automatically encrypted at rest. Google manages the encryption keys by default. Options for customer control: Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) in Cloud KMS, or Customer-Supplied Encryption Keys (CSEK) that you provide directly.

options.name: Google-managed encryption keys
options.description: Default. Google manages keys. AES-256. No configuration needed.

options.name: Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK)
options.description: You manage keys in Cloud KMS. Control key rotation, access, and destruction.

options.name: Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK)
options.description: You provide the key directly. Google does not store it. Must supply key for every operation.

default.note: All persistent disks are encrypted at rest by default with Google-managed keys.

match_nodes.description: Interactive serial console — disabled by default via org policy

data.description: Interactive serial console for troubleshooting boot issues and network misconfigurations. Disabled by default at the organization level via the compute.disableSerialPortAccess constraint. Must be explicitly enabled.

default.note: Disabled by default. Organization policy compute.disableSerialPortAccess enforced.

data.vendor_recommendation: Keep serial port access disabled. Enable temporarily for troubleshooting, then disable when done. Use org policy to enforce across all projects.

match_nodes.description: Identity-Aware Proxy for SSH/RDP without external IP

data.description: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) TCP forwarding allows SSH and RDP connections to instances without external IP addresses. Traffic tunnels through IAP, which verifies the user's identity and IAM permissions before allowing the connection. All connections are logged.
data.vendor_recommendation: Use IAP tunnel instead of external IPs for admin access. Grant roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor to authorized users. Combine with OS Login for end-to-end IAM-based access control.

audit.note: Instances should have no external IP (accessConfigs empty) when using IAP tunnel exclusively.

configure.cli_firewall: gcloud compute firewall-rules create allow-iap-ssh --network {vpc} --allow tcp:22 --source-ranges 35.235.240.0/20
configure.note: IAP uses the source range 35.235.240.0/20. Firewall rules must allow this range.
